data.title: Live updates: Homan addresses Minneapolis situation; Senate tries to keep government open
data.description:
Trump administration border czar Tom Homan will hold a press conference Thursday morning as he assumes command of the immigration crackdown in Minneapolis.

The Senate is also scrambling as a Jan. 30 partial shutdown deadline quickly approaches and Democrats dig their heels in on Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding.

After Saturday’s fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by immigration officials in Minneapolis, the fight over President Trump’s immigration enforcement operations has reached a fever pitch across the country and on Capitol Hill. Some Senate Republicans, however, have said they are open to splitting off the DHS funding from a six-bill package to avoid another shutdown.

Pretti’s killing, as well as the fatal shooting of Renee Good in the same city earlier this month, has called Trump’s position on immigration — which has historically been the president’s strongest issue — into question, as the GOP looks to maintain its majority in this year’s midterm elections.

Trump on Thursday morning will receive an intelligence briefing and host a Cabinet meeting at the White House. He will later join first lady Melania Trump at the premiere of her documentary at the Kennedy Center.

Follow live below.

data.title: Watch live: Homan gives remarks in Minnesota amid turmoil over immigration enforcement
data.description:
Border czar Tom Homan will speak with reporters Thursday morning in Minneapolis, as the city reels following two fatal shootings involving federal immigration authorities.

President Trump sent Homan to Minnesota earlier this week to oversee the administration’s ramped up immigration crackdown in the city after Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino left the area on Tuesday.

The border czar has also met with Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D), which he called “productive.”

Homan’s comments come as the Department of Homeland Security and DHS Secretary Kristi Noem face mounting criticism over its tactics in the North Star State and beyond — with some calling for the agency chief to resign or face impeachment. Lawmakers have also battled over funding for the agency as a partial government shutdown looms.

The press conference is scheduled to begin at 8 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: ICE ceases enhanced operations in Maine after Collins presses Noem
data.description:
Sen. Susan Collins (R-Maine) announced Thursday that Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem has informed her that Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) ceased enhanced operations in Maine, something Collins had repeatedly discussed with Noem in recent days.

“I can report that Secretary Noem has informed me that ICE has ended its enhanced activities in the State of Maine,” the senator wrote in a statement.

“There are currently no ongoing or planned large-scale ICE operations here,” she continued. “I have been urging Secretary Noem and others in the Administration to get ICE to reconsider its approach to immigration enforcement in the state.”

Collins added, “I appreciate the Secretary’s willingness to listen to and consider my recommendations and her personal attention to the situation in Maine.”

The Maine Republican has declined to join some of her Republican colleagues, such as Sens. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) and Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska), who have called for the Department of Homeland Security chief to resign or be fired.

Collins told reporters Monday afternoon that she had spoken to Noem and initially declined to reveal the details of their conversation, but later said she asked her to pause the agency’s surge of immigration enforcement officials to both Maine and Minnesota.

data.title: Online age restrictions are the wrong way to protect children
data.description:
Protecting youths online will be the defining internet policy battle of 2026.  

Last year, Australia began implementing the world’s first ban on social media use for kids under 16. And this year, already, a new Virginia law restricts people to one hour of social media usage unless they prove they are at least 16.

The problem is, banning kids from social media is bad policy and likely unconstitutional. 

Two decades ago, I helped challenge a federal law that required age verification for online content. The government’s goal was protecting children, but we showed that the law would function as a blunt instrument of censorship. After two trips to the Supreme Court, the law was struck down for violating the First Amendment.

I am now watching in alarm as state legislatures from Florida to Utah resurrect the same flawed approach to restricting minors’ access to social media. Congress is also threatening to get in on the action. 

As the mother of two young kids, I am sympathetic to the goals of these laws. But as a lawyer who’s seen this fight before, I’m concerned we’re repeating history — with a Supreme Court that may reach a different conclusion this time.  

It is not just an abstract principle that’s at stake. In a world where most adults and virtually all teenagers use social media, the court may soon determine whether millions of young people can access the platforms where civic life increasingly happens, and whether adults can do so without surrendering their privacy.  

These laws require everyone to verify their age to access sites like Facebook, Instagram and Snapchat. Nearly half of American social media users have utilized the medium for political purposes. Young people are connecting with the people and causes that move them, whether that is local civic groups or national politicians like Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) or former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.). 

While social media does have harmful content, it is also where young people go to learn. On YouTube, teens and tweens can learn about protein synthesis from the Amoeba Sisters, or about Mesopotamia from Hip Hughes.  

As the Supreme Court itself acknowledged, social media platforms are “the principal sources for knowing current events … and otherwise exploring the vast realms of human thought and knowledge.” Yet the new restrictions would exclude minors who do not have parental consent for this vital sphere, as well as adults who do not wish to participate in the burdensome age verification process. 

I understand what drives these laws. Every parent worries about social media’s effects on mental health. But these age restrictions represent what the Supreme Court once described as “[burning] the house to roast the pig.”  

What’s more, age-gating schemes may not even work. The United Kingdom began requiring “highly effective age assurance” this summer for online services likely to be accessed by children. There has been a surge in the use of virtual private networks to evade these restrictions. Determined teens will find workarounds, while the technology will invariably block some adult users.

Similarly, age verification proposals in America threaten to create serious complications. Some require uploading government-issued identification, creating fraud risks while excluding the 2.6 million adult U.S. citizens without photo IDs. Facial scanning systems that estimate age have substantial error rates.

The constitutional problems are more fundamental. These laws condition access to political speech — core First Amendment territory — on identity verification. Young people should be able to engage with the political world they’re about to enter as citizens. Adults should be able to participate in the public square without government-mandated identification. 

The legal landscape is shifting. Last June, the Supreme Court upheld Texas’s age verification law for websites containing sexually explicit content. That case dealt with pornography, but it may signal the court’s willingness to look more favorably on age verification requirements. Then, in August, the Supreme Court declined to halt Mississippi’s law, which covers social media platforms, not pornography sites, offering no explanation. Justice Brett Kavanaugh wrote a solo opinion suggesting such broad social media restrictions are likely unconstitutional, but he’s one voice among nine. 

If the court reviews these laws with a complete factual record — looking at what speech is actually blocked and whether the restrictions actually work — the evidence should point clearly toward striking them down. 

The solution lies not with government-mandated access restrictions but in empowering parents with technical tools that block certain apps or enforce hard limits on screen time. Social supports such as Wait Until Eighth groups, where parents pledge not to give their kids smartphones until they reach eighth grade, are also helping. 

Ultimately, the question isn’t whether protecting children matters. It’s whether we can do so without locking young people, and privacy-conscious adults, out of the spaces where so much of American civic life now happens. We can. And the court should make that clear. 

Catherine Crump is clinical professor and director of the Samuelson Law, Technology and Public Policy Clinic at the University of California Berkeley School of Law.

data.title: Video appears to show altercation between Alex Pretti and federal agents days before his death
data.description:
New video revealed Wednesday appears to show Alex Pretti, the ICU nurse who was shot and killed over the weekend by federal immigration officers in Minneapolis, in an altercation with federal authorities over a week before his death.

The footage, taken on Jan. 13, shows an alleged Pretti hurling expletives at the officers next to their SUV before spitting on it and kicking a taillight twice before it shattered. It’s at this point that an officer leaves the vehicle and grabs the man, before more authorities arrive, throw him to the ground and tackle him.

“Help me, help me,” the man can be heard yelling.

Onlookers are seen yelling at the officers before he was released. What appears to be a handgun can also be seen tucked under the man’s waistband as he continues to yell at the officers.

“Please go home,” he said. “Leave.”

Officers deployed tear gas before they reenter their vehicle and drive off, according to the video.

A Department of Homeland Security (DHS) spokesperson told NewsNation, The Hill’s sister network, that Homeland Security Investigations is looking into the footage.

The video was taken 11 days before Pretti was fatally shot during the Trump administration’s ramped up immigration enforcement operation in the city. A Border Patrol agent and a Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officer were placed on administrative leave following the incident.

Developing.

data.title: Klobuchar launches bid for Minnesota governor
data.description:
Sen. Amy Klobuchar (D-Minn.) on Thursday officially threw her hat in the ring to run for governor of Minnesota.

“Minnesotans, we’ve been through a lot,” she wrote on the social platform X. “And I believe this moment calls for grit, resilience, and faith in each other. I believe we must stand up for what’s right. And fix what’s wrong. Today, I’m announcing my candidacy for Governor.”

The bid comes after Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) announced earlier this month that he will not seek a third term amid scrutiny over his handling of a massive welfare fraud scandal in the North Star State.

Developing.

data.title: Back to the future: Trump's restrictions echo the Immigration Act of 1924
data.description:
This month marks one year since President Trump began rolling out some of the most sweeping immigration restrictions in modern U.S. history, particularly targeting immigrants from developing countries.

The administration has implemented more than 500 policy changes which, taken together, prioritize a immigrants’ country of citizenship over their job skills, family ties or the needs of the U.S. economy.

The scope of these measures is striking. The travel ban has been expanded to 39 countries, affecting roughly one-fifth of the world’s population. The Department of Homeland Security has effectively halted processing immigration applications from those countries. The green card lottery has been suspended. Refugee admissions are projected to fall from about 125,000 annually to just 7,500 — the lowest level since the modern refugee program began. And most recently, the U.S. has suspended green card processing at consulates in 75 countries. A USA Today map shows the countries affected are heavily weighted away from western Europe.

If there were any doubt about the administration’s intent, Trump dispelled it in a speech last month in Pennsylvania, when he invoked language about keeping people out from what he once called “s—hole countries.” These policies and statements are not unprecedented. They echo a darker chapter of U.S. immigration history: the national-origin quota system enacted a century ago.

Today, more than 50 million people living in the U.S. are foreign-born — just over 15 percent of the population. The last time the country saw immigration at this scale was in the early 1900s. History suggests that such periods often produce cultural and political backlash.

Congress responded to increased immigration from Southern and Eastern Europe in the 1910s by passing the Immigration Act of 1924. That law sharply curtailed immigration by imposing quotas based on the 1890 census — deliberately favoring immigrants from Northern and Western Europe. Immigration fell by roughly 85 percent, with most new arrivals coming from the United Kingdom, Ireland, Germany and Scandinavia. That policy still reverberates in today’s debates.

Notably, Congress passed the 1924 Act during a period of relative economic strength. It was not primarily a response to unemployment or wage pressures, but, as scholars have noted, “a political remedy resulting from social concerns.” The resulting drop in immigration reduced demand for housing and consumer goods in cities where immigrants had settled, amplifying other negative economic trends such as high tariffs, declining investment and a deepening farm crisis.

Subsequent scholarship has underscored the costs. One law review article found that the immigration restrictions of the 1920s contributed to the severity of the Great Depression. Other research documented a decline in scientific innovation following the 1924 law. More recently, the National Academies of Science, Engineering and Medicine have detailed the productivity losses that would result from cutting high-skilled immigration today.

By the early 1960s, nearly 80 percent of the small number of immigrants admitted to the U.S. came from the United Kingdom, Ireland and Germany. Congress finally reversed course with the Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965. Signing it into law, President Lyndon Johnson declared that America would welcome people “because of what they are, and not because of the land from which they sprung.” The law abolished national-origin quotas and emphasized family unity and employment-based admissions.

Even so, the current system remains deeply flawed. Only about 16 percent of green cards — roughly 140,000 a year in the world’s largest economy — are allocated through employment-based categories. And that number includes spouses and children — a single Ph.D.-level engineer arriving with a spouse and two children consumes four of those green cards.

The immigration system clearly needs reform. But reform should not mean reverting to 1924 and selecting immigrants based on birthplace rather than potential. As President Ronald Reagan said in his farewell address, “We lead the world because, unique among nations, we draw our people — our strength — from every country and every corner of the world.” Reagan urged the country to look forward, not backward: “While other countries cling to the stale past, here in America we breathe life into dreams.”

The current administration’s policy restrictions choke off immigration from developing countries, reflecting the worst of the 1924 Act. And the administration’s mass deportation and enforcement effort goes well beyond the 1924 restrictions on new incoming immigrants. The U.S. economy needs the vitality, innovation, investment, and consumer spending of immigrants from every country.

There are many thoughtful ideas to start a conversation on immigration priorities, such as a plan proposed last month by a group of former government officials, a white paper by some of our Cornell immigration fellows, and Rep. Tom Suozzi’s (D-N.Y.) bipartisan proposal. Nonpartisan groups like Braver Angels are also convening conversations to look for areas of agreement.

Trump’s immigration restrictions have bypassed Congress. Let’s hope Congress can fix our broken immigration system to focus immigration policy on the needs of the country and our 21st century economy.

Dan Berger is a partner at Green and Spiegel and a nonresident academic fellow at Cornell Law School. Stephen Yale-Loehr is a retired immigration law professor at Cornell Law School. The authors thank Professor Tom Guglielmo of George Washington University for his insights on U.S. immigration history.

data.title: Ford and GM get approval to launch banks: What it means for you
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Ford and GM are one step closer to launching their own banks, a move that could eventually give car shoppers more competitive financing options.

Federal regulators recently granted conditional approval for the companies to set up industrial banks that will focus on providing auto financing products nationwide, according to the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC).

The model is appealing to automakers because it lets them fund loans directly through customer deposits rather than the costlier forms of borrowing that they rely on today.

“By creating these entities, it should give them access to a much lower cost of funds, which ultimately can pass on to their future customers,” said Patrick Roosenberg, senior director of auto finance at J.D. Power.

Ford said on its website that the approval will allow it to introduce “additional savings options” to customers and plans to offer products like certificates of deposit through a digital-first bank. GM said its new bank will complement its existing financial services arm by providing “stable and diversified funding” through deposit products.

The approval comes as affordability remains a major pressure point in the new-vehicle market. The average price paid for a new car topped $50,000 for the first time last September, according to Kelley Blue Book, and many buyers are leaning on longer loan terms to keep payments manageable.

In theory, Ford and GM’s new banks should give the automakers more financing flexibility at a time when traditional banks have been gaining market share in the new-car loan market.

“I think it can truly benefit all three parties — the dealer, the lender, and the end consumer,” Roosenberg said. “More competition should bring out more competitive rates with more options.”

Critics caution against mixing banking and commerce

Industrial banks, also known as industrial loan companies, have long drawn pushback from regulators and policy experts who warn they blur the line between banking and commerce.

“The separation of banking and commerce is there for a reason: to avoid conflicts of interest,” said Thomas Hoenig, a distinguished senior fellow at the Mercatus Center who was vice chairman of the FDIC from 2012 to 2018.

The core worry is that a company with its own bank could use its lending power to favor itself or its affiliates, undermining fair competition. Critics also warn that companies could be tempted to make risky loans to boost sales.

The Independent Community Bankers of America, a community bank trade group, opposed the FDIC’s recent approval, arguing that the move could pose risks to the broader banking system.

Back in 2006, the FDIC imposed a moratorium on new ILC applications after intense opposition to Walmart’s effort to set up an industrial bank — a bid the retailer ultimately withdrew.

There are currently 24 industrial banks in the U.S., according to the agency. But after years of hesitancy toward new applications, Hoenig said the recent approvals suggest regulators are more open to the idea than they used to be.

It’s a posture he has long opposed.

“I always thought it was a bad idea,” Hoenig said.

It remains to be seen whether the latest approvals will lead regulators to sign off on other applications. Stellantis and Nissan applied for industrial loan company charters last year, while Toyota and BMW already operate industrial banks in the U.S.

Ford and GM’s industrial banks — Ford Credit Bank and GM Financial Bank — will both be chartered in Utah. The companies have 12 months to establish them before the FDIC’s conditional approval expires.

data.title: Indonesia ends elephant rides nationwide
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Tourists looking to ride elephants in Indonesia will no longer be able to do so as the country’s government has officially banned the practice.

The decision to end the popular tourism attraction throughout the entire archipelago is being applauded by animal rights activists who have long condemned the practice. World Animal Protection reports that in December, the Ministry of Forestry’s Directorate General of Natural Resources and Ecosystem Conservation issued Circular Letter No. 6 of 2025, formally ending elephant riding at all conservation and tourism facilities across the country.

According to the nonprofit, facilities that previously offered elephant rides are being directed to shift its tourism toward ethical, observation-based experiences. Those who fail to comply risk having their operating permits revoked. Mason Elephant Park in Bali, one of the final venues in Indonesia to offer elephant rides, ceased the activity Jan. 25 after receiving formal warnings, according to Australian Leisure Management.

“We congratulate the Indonesian Government on taking this world-leading step to safeguarding the dignity of wild animals,” Suzanne Milthorpe, head of campaigns for World Animal Protection, wrote. “The ending of elephant rides in Indonesia sends a strong signal to the wider tourism industry that we’re entering a new chapter of more responsible wildlife tourism.”

The organization and other animal rights groups, such as PETA, have issued numerous reports over the years detailing harmful treatment and training practices behind elephant rides. These tactics have ranged from physical abuse to the withholding of food and veterinary care.

data.title: Trump is sleepwalking into a forever-war in Europe
data.description:
Tragically, we have witnessed this trainwreck coming for months. Former President Joe Biden’s “just enough” strategy of aiding Ukraine in its resistance of a Russian invasion now risks becoming President Trump’s forever-war in Europe.

As Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky left Davos following his meeting with Trump last week, he told reporters that a document with the U.S. dealing concerning security guarantees for Ukraine was “done.” Zelensky emphasized that “For us, security guarantees are first and foremost guarantees from the United States. The document is 100 percent ready, and we are waiting for our partners to confirm the date and place for its signing.”

Trump added that a settlement for the Ukraine war “is coming very soon.”

Special Envoy Steve Witkoff and Trump’s son-in-law Jared Kushner sped off to Moscow to brief Russian President Vladimir Putin, then proceeded to the United Arab Emirates to participate in the trilateral talks.

The talks commenced and concluded in Abu Dhabi the next day. The outcome? Russia refused to back off their maximalist demands and continued to demand Ukraine unilaterally withdraw from the Donbas.

The U.S. is now 0 for 7 in its negotiations with Russia to end the war — chiefly because Ukraine stubbornly refuses to commit national suicide.

Kyiv will not give Moscow in negotiations what the Russians cannot take on the battlefield. Nor should they be persuaded or coerced into doing so. 

The Fortress Belt in the Donetsk Oblast is where Ukraine has drawn the line. It is key terrain, their no-penetration line. It is essential to the defense of Ukraine — protecting key avenues of approach towards Kyiv and Odesa.

In the Donbas, Team Zelensky has adopted the U.S. Army motto: “This we’ll defend!” Embracing the U.S. Army mission to “safeguard the nation, protect its citizenry, and uphold its values.” 

Secretary of the Army Daniel P. Driscoll joined Witkoff and Kushner in the trilateral talks. Do you suppose that resonated with the former Army officer as they applied pressure upon Ukraine to cede the Donbas to Russia?

On Tuesday, Trump pulled a 180. The security guarantees he offered were contingent upon Ukraine withdrawing from the Donbas. That is a hard no for Zelensky and for the Ukrainian people.

On the legal side, Ukrainian law prevents holding a referendum on issues that could threaten the country’s territorial integrity. Then there is public opinion. A poll conducted by the Kyiv International Institute of Sociology in December revealed that 75 percent of Ukrainians “rejected a proposed peace plan that would involve the withdrawal of Ukrainian forces from Donbas.”

The harder Russia pushes back, the harder the Trump administration appears to come back and lean on Ukraine. It is just a matter of time before Trump will again accuse Zelensky of “not wanting peace” and being an “obstructionist.”

Russia wants to make a deal with the U.S., only to secure that deal, Trump needs Zelensky to stop resisting. 

For now, the sticking point is the Donbas. But what if Ukraine agreed to withdraw from the region? Would Russia end the war? Probably not. It will just lead to the next bone of contention.

Three come to mind: As it has in the past, Russia will balk about weapons systems provided to Ukraine for its defense, the physical presence of NATO or European Forces as peacekeepers, and the size of the Ukrainian military.

And while we wait for the ‘next round’ of negotiations to take place, Russia will continue to strike Ukrainian cities and their energy infrastructure with ballistic missiles and drones.

As we warned three years ago, Putin has weaponized winter. Despite this and other war crimes he has committed, he is unlikely to be held accountable despite an arrest warrant issued by the International Criminal Court.

Especially since Team Trump wants to quickly end the war so Washington can secure economic opportunities.

Putin’s military cannot win on the battlefield. It has suffered more than 1.2 million casualties to date. Instead, he is waging a terror war against unarmed civilians in their homes, killing 6,576 and wounding 27,931 since 2023.

We assess it may take three to five years to secure the Donbas at a cost of hundreds of thousands of additional Russian soldiers. They just sustained 415,000 casualties during the first year of Trump’s second term.

Putin is the ‘root cause’ of the war. The killing stops when Putin stops attacking. 

Witkoff’s negotiations have done nothing to bring the war to an end. They only prolong it, creating a “forever-war.”

Trump has the cards to end the war, but those cards need to be played against Russia and not Ukraine. He must coerce Russia to stop attacking, give up their territorial aspirations for the Donbas, and accept a European military peace-keeping force in Ukraine. 

That will likely require military force. It begins with a NATO-enforced no-fly zone over western Ukraine, sufficiently arming Kyiv to defeat Russian forces in Ukraine, and destroying Moscow’s ability to fund and sustain the war. 

Anything less equals a Team Trump forever war in Europe.

Col. (Ret.) Jonathan Sweet served 30 years as a military intelligence officer and led the U.S. European Command Intelligence Engagement Division from 2012 to 2014. Mark Toth writes on national security and foreign policy.
